2603.19744.title: Rethinking Ground Truth: A Case Study on Human Label Variation in MLLM Benchmarking
2603.19744.paper_summary: Human Label Variation (HLV), i.e. systematic differences among annotators' judgments, remains underexplored in benchmarks despite rapid progress in large language model (LLM) development. We address this gap by introducing an evaluation protocol for multimodal large language model (MLLM) benchmarking that explicitly accounts for two conditions: (1) human label agreement and (2) disagreement. We apply this protocol to two state-of-the-art MLLM families (Gemma 3, Qwen 2.5 VL) using non-aggregated human annotations from a social media content classification dataset. Across tasks, we find that larger models tend to perform best on high-agreement subsets, yet often underperform medium-sized models when human disagreement is high, indicating that parameter count alone does not determine sensitivity to ambiguity and subjectivity. These results show that benchmarks based solely on consensus labels can overstate model capabilities in such domains and that incorporating human label variation yields more realistic and robust assessments of MLLMs in content moderation pipelines.
2603.19744.paper_summary_zh: 人類標籤變異（HLV），即標註者判斷之間的系統性差異，在基準測試中仍未得到充分探索，儘管大型語言模型（LLM）發展迅速。<lb/>我們通過引入一種多模態大型語言模型（MLLM）基準測試的評估協議來填補這一空白，該協議明確考慮了兩個條件：（1）人類標籤一致性和（2）不一致性。<lb/>我們將這一協議應用於兩個最先進的MLLM系列（Gemma 3，Qwen 2.5 VL），使用來自社交媒體內容分類數據集的非聚合人類標註。<lb/>在各項任務中，我們發現較大的模型在高一致性子集上表現最佳，但在高人類不一致性時，往往表現不及中型模型，這表明僅僅依賴參數數量並不能決定對模糊性和主觀性的敏感性。<lb/>這些結果顯示，僅基於共識標籤的基準測試可能會高估模型在這些領域的能力，而納入人類標籤變異則能提供更現實和穩健的MLLM評估，特別是在內容審核流程中。
2603.19744.author: Tomas Ruiz et.al.
2603.19744.authors: Tomas Ruiz, Tanalp Agustoslu, Carsten Schwemmer
